annotations.label: Operator (general)

anchor.snippet: an operator is generally a mapping or function that acts on elements of a space to produce elements of another space

annotations.note: The generic notion of an "operator" as just a mapping between spaces has no dedicated formalization; it is simply a function in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Linear operator

anchor.snippet: Linear operators refer to linear maps whose domain and range are the same space

annotations.note: A linear map whose domain and range coincide is exactly `Module.End R M`, the type of linear endomorphisms.

annotations.label: Differential and integral operators

anchor.snippet: differentiation and indefinite integration are linear operators

annotations.note: Differentiation is bundled as a continuous linear operator on Schwartz space (`derivCLM`) and integration linearity holds (`intervalIntegral.integral_add`), but there is no general bundled differentiation/indefinite-integration operator.

annotations.label: Linear mapping

anchor.snippet: for all x and y in U , and for all α , β in K

annotations.note: The additivity-plus-homogeneity defining property is exactly `LinearMap` (bundled) and `IsLinearMap` (unbundled predicate) in the same file.

anchors.snippet: for all x and y in U , and for all α , β in K

annotations.label: Matrix representation of linear operators

anchor.snippet: matrices are in bijective correspondence to linear operators

annotations.note: `LinearMap.toMatrix` is a linear equivalence between linear maps and matrices given chosen bases, formalizing the bijective correspondence.

anchors.snippet: matrices are in bijective correspondence to linear operators

annotations.label: Sequence spaces

anchor.snippet: these spaces, together with linear subspaces, are known as sequence spaces

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes the ℓᵖ sequence spaces as `lp E p` (with predicate `Memℓp`), a special case of the general notion of sequence spaces.

annotations.label: Sequence transformations

anchor.snippet: Operators on these spaces are known as sequence transformations

annotations.note: There is no dedicated "sequence transformation" notion; such maps are just (continuous) linear maps on `lp`.

annotations.label: Bounded operators form a Banach algebra

anchor.snippet: Bounded linear operators over a Banach space form a Banach algebra in respect to the standard operator norm

annotations.note: The operator norm makes `E →L[𝕜] E` a `NormedRing` (and `NormedAlgebra`), and it is complete when `E` is, giving the Banach-algebra structure (no single `BanachAlgebra` class is used).

annotations.label: Bounded operator

anchor.snippet: a linear operator from U to V is called bounded if there exists c > 0 such that

annotations.note: Bounded linear operators are formalized as `ContinuousLinearMap`, with `LinearMap.continuous_of_bound` giving the bound characterization in normed spaces.

anchors.snippet: a linear operator from U to V is called bounded if there exists c > 0 such that

annotations.label: Bounded operators form a vector space

anchor.snippet: Bounded operators form a vector space

annotations.note: Continuous (bounded) linear maps carry `AddCommGroup` and `Module` instances, so they form a vector space.

anchors.snippet: Bounded operators form a vector space

annotations.label: Banach algebra

anchor.snippet: Any unital normed algebra with this property is called a Banach algebra

annotations.note: Mathlib has `NormedAlgebra`/`NormedRing` but no dedicated `BanachAlgebra` class; the Banach condition is expressed by adding `CompleteSpace`.

annotations.label: Differential and Volterra operators

anchor.snippet: calculus is the study of two linear operators

annotations.note: The differential operator is formalized as a CLM on Schwartz space and integration is linear, but the Volterra (indefinite integration) operator is not formalized as such.

annotations.label: Gradient operator

anchor.section: Fundamental analysis operators on scalar and vector fields
anchor.snippet: assigns a vector at every point in a scalar field that points in the direction of greatest rate of change

annotations.note: `gradient f x` is defined as the Riesz dual of `fderiv`, formalizing the gradient of a scalar field (with `HasGradientAt`).

annotations.label: Divergence operator

anchor.section: Fundamental analysis operators on scalar and vector fields
anchor.snippet: is a vector operator that measures a vector field's divergence from or convergence towards a given point

annotations.note: No divergence operator on vector fields is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Curl operator

anchor.section: Fundamental analysis operators on scalar and vector fields
anchor.snippet: measures a vector field's curling

annotations.note: No curl operator on vector fields is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Invertible operators form the general linear group

anchor.snippet: bijective operators preserving the structure of a vector space are precisely the invertible linear operators

annotations.note: `LinearMap.GeneralLinearGroup R M` is defined as the units `(M →ₗ[R] M)ˣ`, i.e. the group of invertible linear operators.

annotations.label: Invertible operators not closed under addition

anchor.snippet: they do not form a vector space under operator addition

annotations.note: The specific fact that the general linear group is not closed under operator addition is not stated in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Isometry and orthogonal groups

anchor.snippet: Operators preserving the Euclidean metric on such a space form the isometry group

annotations.note: The linear operators preserving the Euclidean metric form `Matrix.orthogonalGroup`; the full affine isometry group (including translations) is not packaged as a single named group.

annotations.label: Special orthogonal group

anchor.snippet: Operators in the orthogonal group that also preserve the orientation of vector tuples form the special orthogonal group

annotations.note: `Matrix.specialOrthogonalGroup` is defined as the orthogonal matrices with determinant one, matching the orientation-preserving subgroup.

annotations.label: Covariance as a dot product

anchor.snippet: every covariance is basically a dot product

annotations.note: `covarianceBilin` formalizes covariance as a continuous (inner-product-like) bilinear form on a Banach space, capturing the dot-product viewpoint.

annotations.label: Fourier series representation of periodic functions

anchor.snippet: any continuous periodic function can be represented as the sum of a series of sine waves and cosine waves

annotations.note: Mathlib proves L² convergence of the Fourier series (`hasSum_fourier_series_L2`, via `fourierBasis`) and pointwise convergence under summability, but not the general continuous-pointwise representation statement.

anchors.snippet: any continuous periodic function can be represented as the sum of a series of sine waves and cosine waves

anchors.value: {\displaystyle f(t)={\frac {\ a_{0}\ }{2}}+\sum _{n=1}^{\infty }\ a_{n}\cos(\omega \ n\ t)+b_{n}\sin(\omega \ n\ t)}

annotations.label: Fourier series is a linear operator

anchor.snippet: Fourier series is a linear operator

annotations.note: Linearity of the Fourier-coefficient map is established (`fourierCoeff.add`, `fourierCoeff.const_smul`), but it is not bundled as a single `LinearMap`.

anchors.snippet: Fourier series is a linear operator

anchors.value: {\displaystyle f(t)={\frac {\ a_{0}\ }{2}}+\sum _{n=1}^{\infty }\ a_{n}\cos(\omega \ n\ t)+b_{n}\sin(\omega \ n\ t)}

annotations.label: Fourier transform (integral form)

anchor.snippet: the transform takes on an integral form

annotations.note: `VectorFourier.fourierIntegral` defines the Fourier transform in integral form (with `Real.fourierIntegral` the one-dimensional case).

anchors.snippet: the transform takes on an integral form

annotations.label: Laplace transform

anchor.snippet: Given f = f ( s ) , it is defined by

annotations.note: No Laplace transform is defined in Mathlib (only the related Mellin transform exists).

anchors.snippet: Given f = f ( s ) , it is defined by

annotations.label: Linear operator on V

anchor.snippet: A linear transformation from V to V is called a linear operator on V

annotations.note: A linear transformation from V to V is exactly `Module.End`, the linear endomorphisms of V.

annotations.label: Real and complex operators

anchor.snippet: A linear operator on a real vector space is called a real operator

annotations.note: This is terminology distinguishing linear operators by scalar field (ℝ vs ℂ); Mathlib has `LinearMap` over each field but no "real operator"/"complex operator" notion.
